facts: Evaporation is the process where liquid water turns into water vapor due to heat from the sun; this water vapor rises and is the first step in the water cycle that eventually leads to clouds and precipitation.
Condensation occurs when water vapor in the air cools and changes back into liquid droplets; these droplets gather to form clouds, so without condensation, clouds would not exist.
Precipitation happens when cloud droplets become heavy enough to fall as rain, snow, sleet, or hail; the type of precipitation depends on the temperature inside the cloud and the air below it.

Climate is the long-term average of weather conditions (temperature, precipitation, etc.) over at least 30 years, not the weather on a single day; for example, a desert has a dry climate even if it rains occasionally.

Latitude and oceans both affect climate: regions near the equator are warmer because they receive more direct sunlight, and coastal areas have milder temperatures than inland areas at the same latitude because water heats and cools more slowly than land.

Altitude affects climate: higher elevations are cooler than lower elevations at the same latitude because the air is thinner and holds less heat; this is why mountain peaks are often snow-capped even in tropical regions.
misconceptions: Climate and weather are the same thing; a rainy day means the region has a rainy climate.
Clouds are made of water vapor, not liquid water droplets.
Higher elevations are warmer because they are closer to the sun.
